title: Local AI Agents Masterclass (Gemma 4 + OpenCode + Odysseus tutorial)
summary: Jordan Urbs demonstrates setting up Odysseus (a workspace agent framework) and OpenCode (a build agent) for local execution using Gemma 4 via Ollama, with Venice API as a fallback provider. The tutorial covers installation in Docker, model management, agent skills and memory systems, and a hybrid approach of building with frontier models then executing tasks locally.
writeup: This video introduces two distinct paradigms for running AI agents locally: an out-of-the-box agentic workspace (Odysseus, installed via Docker) and purpose-built custom agent pipelines for content creation. The central architectural argument is a tiered inference strategy: use a small, cheap, locally-run open-source model (Gemma 4 12B at 7.4 GB VRAM) for routine execution, and route to a privacy-preserving remote API (Venice) only when a larger frontier model is needed for high-stakes authoring. The through-line is practical privacy: mainstream providers log and train on your prompts, while Venice offers zero-data-retention contracts for agentic model tiers and acts as a privacy proxy for anonymized calls.

key_points: Odysseus (60,000-star GitHub repo) runs as a Docker container or native binary and supports any model provider through a unified interface, making it the recommended first stop for developers unfamiliar with self-hosted agent infrastructure.

Gemma 4 12B requires only 7.4 GB of RAM under quantization and is presented as matching reasoning quality previously associated with models needing 30 GB, making capable local inference accessible on a 16 GB consumer machine via Ollama.

Venice distinguishes between two privacy tiers on its agentic model list: 'private' (contractual zero-data-retention with the underlying GPU provider) and 'anonymized' (Venice acts as a proxy so the provider never sees identifying context), offering a concrete mechanism for using frontier APIs without logging.

The teacher/executor cost split is the core economic argument: a frontier model such as Claude Opus 4.8 at $30 per million output tokens authors or repairs the workflow once, then a cheap executor such as Kimi K2 at $4.66 per million tokens runs it repeatedly, cutting ongoing inference cost by roughly 6x.

Connecting Ollama to Odysseus running inside Docker requires manually finding the host machine's LAN IP address and pasting it as a custom endpoint, because the container cannot resolve 'localhost' to the host OS where Ollama is listening.
